title: Port Auth. of Guam v. Civ. Serv. Comm'n - Decision and Order
text:
\
2026 APR -3 PM 4: 57
By:_+-
2
CLERH OF COUin
3
4
5
IN THE SUPERIOR COURT OF GUAM
6
7 PORT AUTHORITY OF GUAM, SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS NO. SP0l 19-24
8
Petitioner,
9
DECISION AND ORDER
V.
10
CIVIL SERVICE COMMISSION,
II
12 Respondent,
13
and
14
15 JOYJEAN R. ARCEO,
16
Real Party
17
In Interest.
18
INTRODUCTION
19
This case turns on who bore the burden of proof-and what the law requires when that
20
burden is not met. After the Port Authority of Guam discharged a classified employee, the Civil
21
Service Commission heard the appeal, applied the governing standard, and deliberated. The
22
Commission split evenly specifically two commissioners voted to sustain the Port's adverse
23
action and two commissioners voted to revoke it. Under Guam law, that division did not
24
preserve the adverse action or render the decision void; it meant the Port failed to persuade the
25
four commissioners required to prevail. The Port now asks this Court to declare the
26
Commission's decision void. The law does not permit that result. The burden remained on
27
management, the burden was not met, and the Commission acted within its authority in granting
28

Decision and Order
SPOI 19-24; Port Authority of Guam v. Civil Service Commission, Joyjean R. Arceo
relief. Attorney James Canto represents the Port Authority of Guam and Attorney Jacqueline
2 Terlaje represents Real Party in Interest Joyjean R. Arceo.
3
BACKGROUND
4
5
The Port Authority of Guam ("Port") petitions for judicial review of a Civil Service
6
Commission ("CSC") Decision and Judgment dated August 6, 2024 concerning the terminatio
7
of Port employee JoyJean R. Arceo (Arceo). The Port terminated Arceo, a classified employe
8
serving as Port Procurement and Supply Manager, by Final Notice of Adverse Action effectiv
9
10 October 30, 2023, citing refusal to perform prescribed duties, insubordination, and unsatisfactor
11
performance. See, Verified Pet. (Sep. 5, 2024). Arceo appealed the termination to the CSC o
12
November 20, 2023. Id. at 2.
13
Before the merits hearing, the CSC determined that the Port's allegations could constitute
14
15 Official Misconduct under 9 GCA § 49.90 if charged and reduced the Port's burden of proof to
16 substantial evidence pursuant to 4 GCA § 4407(c). Id. at 6-10. Following deliberations on Jun
17
12, 2024, the Commission split evenly, with two commissioners voting to sustain the Port'
18
adverse action and two voting to revoke it. Id. at 4-5. The CSC stated that because Managemen
19
failed to obtain four votes to sustain the termination, the Employee prevailed. Id. The Po
20
21 objected on the record, asserting that the Commission lacked authority to revoke the adverse
22
action absent the "affirmative vote of four (4) members" required by 4 GCA § 4402. Id. at 13.
23
On August 6, 2024, the CSC issued a written Decision and Judgment awarding relief i
24
Arceo's favor, including back pay and benefits, restoration of leave, retirement contributions,
25
and attorney's fees and costs pursuant to 4 GCA § 4406.1. Id. at 2-3. The Port moved fo
26
27 reconsideration on August 9, 2024, arguing the CSC could not lawfully take action without fou
28
Page 2 of7

Decision and Order
SPOJ 19-24; Port Authority of Guam v. Civil Service Commission, Joyjean R. Arceo
affirmative votes. Id. at 17. The CSC denied reconsideration by a 4-0 vote on August 27, 2024.
Id.
2
3 The Port filed this petition for judicial review on September 5, 2024 and the Court too
4
this matter under advisement on January 9, 2026.
5
6
DISCUSSION
7
The Court proceeds in four steps. First, the Court addresses the statutory "Rule of Four"
8
9 in 4 GCA § 4402 and its consistent interpretation by reviewing courts. Second, the Cou
10 explains how that rule operates in adverse action appeals, where management bears the burden o
11
proof, and why a split vote means that burden was not met. Third, the Court rejects the Port's
12
assertion that a divided Commission acts without authority, explaining why that theory is bot
13
legally incorrect and practically untenable. Finally, the Court considers legislative intent,
14
15 including Public Law 26-88, which confirms that the statutory scheme was designed to
16
increase-rather than dilute-management's burden in adverse action proceedings.
17
18 I. The Rule of Four Governs Civil Service Commission Action.
19
This case turns on the meaning and operation of 4 GCA § 4402. The statute provides tha
20
21 "[t]he quorum of the Commission shall be four (4) members" and that "[t]he affirmative vote o
22
four (4) members shall be required for any action of the Commission." The Appellate Divisio
23
has squarely construed this language to require the concurrence of four votes, not merely the
24
participation of four members. In Government of Guam, Department ofP ublic Health and Socia
25
26 Services v. Civil Service Commission, Dennis Quinones, and the Commission voted 3-1 t
27 uphold an employee's termination but reinstated the employee because the government failed to
28
persuade four commissioners. 1987 WL 109892, 1-3 (D. Guam App. Div. Aug. 27, 1987). Th
Page 3 of7

Decision and Order
SPOl 19-24; Port Authority of Guam v. Civil Service Commission, Joyjean R. Arceo
court affirmed, holding that § 4402 does not permit action by a simple majority of a quorum an
that failure to obtain four concurring votes means the party bearing the burden has not prevailed.
2
3 The Ninth Circuit reaffirmed this construction in Guam Power Authority v. Civil Servic
4
Commission, explaining that under the "Rule of Four," when the party with the burden of proo
5
does not receive four votes for its position, the opposing party is entitled to a decision in it
6
favor. 967 F.2d 586, 1 (9th Cir. 1992).
7
8 The Supreme Court of Guam has likewise recognized the Rule of Four, stating that while
9 it declined to reach compliance on the facts because the issue was raised for the first time o
10
appeal, it expressed "no quarrel" with the Rule of Four principle. Limtiaco v. Guam Fire Dep 't,
II
2007 Guam 10 ,i 65.
12
The Port argues that the CSC lacked authority to grant relief because fewer than fou
13
14 commissioners voted to revoke the adverse action. That argument mistakes both § 4402 and the
15
allocation of the burden of proof in adverse action appeals. Section 4402 does not preserve a
16
adverse action by default when the Commission is divided. Rather, it places the risk of non
17
persuasion squarely on the party bearing the burden. Where management fails to secure the fou
18
concurring votes required by statute, it has not carried that burden. In that circumstance, th
19
20 Commission does not exceed its authority by granting relief; it applies the statute as written.
21
II. A Split Vote Means the Party with the Burden Did Not Prevail.
22
23
The Rule of Four does not exist m isolation. It operates within the adverse actio
24
framework, where management bears the burden of proof. When management fails to secure
25
26 four affirmative votes, it has not carried that burden.
27
28
Page 4 of7

Decision and Order
SPOI 19-24; Port Authority of Guam v. Civil Service Commission, Joyjean R. Arceo
This principle was reaffirmed by the Ninth Circuit in Guam Power Authority, whic
upheld the CSC's Rule of Four practice and rejected a challenge materially identical to the on
2
3 raised here. The court explained that when the party with the burden of proof does not receive
4
four votes, "the opposing party is entitled to a decision in its favor."
5
The Port's position would invert this structure by allowing management to prevail b
6
stalemate. Nothing in § 4402 authorizes that outcome. To the contrary, Quinones and Guam
7
Power Authority make clear that a tie vote is not neutrality-it is failure of proof by the part
8
9 who bears the burden.
IO
Here, the CSC deliberated and split evenly. Because sustaining the adverse actio
II
required four affirmative votes, the Port failed to carry its burden. The Commission correctl
12
treated that failure as dispositive and entered judgment in favor of the employee.
13
14
III. The Port's "Void Decision" Theory Is Impractical and Contrary to Adjudicativ
15
Principles
16
The Port asks this Court to declare the CSC's Decision void whenever fewer than fou
17
commissioners vote to revoke an adverse action. That remedy is neither compelled by statute no
18
workable in practice.
19
20 The CSC may reach unanimity, a majority, or a split decision after independen
21
consideration of the evidence. The law does not-and cannot-require commissioners to vote i
22
any particular manner. A divided commission is not an anomaly; it is a foreseeable outcome o
23
adjudication.
24
The Port's interpretation would convert every split vote into legal paralysis, effective!
25
26 forcing the Commission to reach unanimity or risk having its proceedings nullified. Nothing i
27
Title 4 suggests the Legislature intended such a result.
28
Page 5 of7

Decision and Order
SPOI 19-24; Port Authority of Guam v. Civil Service Commission, Joyjean R. Arceo
More fundamentally, the Port conflates voting mechanics with burden of proof. A spli
vote does not mean the Commission acted without authority. It means the party bearing th
2
3 burden failed to persuade the decision-makers required by law.
4
The statutory framework mirrors a familiar adjudicative principle. In a jury trial requirin
5
a unanimous verdict, a deadlocked jury does not result in judgment for the party with the burde
6
by default or a verdict void. Rather, the party with the burden has failed to obtain the verdic
7
required by law. The Rule of Four operates in the same manner. The statute does not compel fou
8
9 commissioners to vote in any direction. It defines the number of votes required for a party to
10
prevail. When that threshold is not met, the consequence flows from failure of persuasion, no
11
from procedural defect.
12
Accepting the Port's position would allow management to prevail not by proof, but b
13
14 deadlock. The Legislature did not design the civil service system to reward stalemate.
15
IV. The Legislature Reinforced This Result in Public Law 26-88.
16
17
The Legislature's reenactment of§ 4402 in Public Law 26-88 confirms this reading. I
18
amending the statute, the Legislature expressly found that the prior law was "unacceptable in
19
20 meting out justice and highly unfavorable to the employees," and that the number of votes
21
required to "affirmatively carry out the decision of the Commission must be increased." P.L. 26-
22
88, § 1.
23
The Legislature simultaneously increased management's burden of proof and articulate
24
an intent to "level the playing field" between management and employees. Nothing in the
25
26 legislative findings suggests intent to allow management to prevail by default when th
27
28
Page 6 of7

Decision and Order
SPOJ 19-24; Port Authority of Guam v. Civil Service Commission, Joyjean R. Arceo
Commission is divided. To the contrary, the amendments were designed to make it mor
difficult-not easier-for management to sustain adverse actions.
2
3 Adopting the Port's interpretation would undermine that purpose by allowing adverse
4
actions to stand without the affirmative concurrence required by statute. The Court declines to
5
adopt a construction that conflicts with clear legislative intent.
6
7 CONCLUSION
8 Accordingly, the Port's Petition for Judicial Review is DENIED.
9

HONORABLE ARTHUR R. BARCINAS
